questions.question: The American Declaration of Independence was signed in which year?
questions.explanation: The Declaration of Independence was adopted by the Continental Congress on July 4, 1776, in Philadelphia. Written primarily by Thomas Jefferson, it declared the 13 American colonies independent from British rule and outlined the philosophical foundations of American democracy, including the principles that 'all men are created equal' and have inalienable rights to life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness.
questions.memory_hook: 1776 = independence. July 4th. Jefferson wrote it in about 17 days. The document that started a nation also started a revolution in how governments justify their power.

questions.question: Which ancient Egyptian queen was famously linked with both Julius Caesar and Mark Antony?
questions.explanation: Cleopatra VII (69-30 BC) was the last active ruler of the Ptolemaic Kingdom of Egypt. She formed powerful political and romantic alliances with Julius Caesar and, after his assassination, with Mark Antony. She was known for her intelligence and command of multiple languages — she was reportedly the first Ptolemaic ruler to speak Egyptian. She died by suicide after Octavian defeated Antony.
questions.memory_hook: Cleopatra = Caesar + Mark Antony. She spoke 9 languages and charmed the two most powerful men in Rome. Closer in time to the Moon landing than to the pyramids.

questions.question: What was the name of the first artificial satellite launched into space, sent by the Soviet Union in 1957?
questions.explanation: Sputnik 1 was launched by the Soviet Union on October 4, 1957, becoming the first artificial satellite to orbit Earth. It was a metal sphere about the size of a beach ball that transmitted a simple radio beep. The launch shocked the United States and triggered the Space Race, leading directly to the creation of NASA and, eventually, the Apollo Moon landings.
questions.memory_hook: Sputnik = first satellite, 1957. A beach ball beeping in space started the Space Race. Americans heard it on the radio and panicked. NASA was created in response.

questions.question: The Hundred Years' War was fought between which two countries?
questions.explanation: The Hundred Years' War (1337-1453) was a series of conflicts between England and France over claims to the French throne. It actually lasted 116 years. Joan of Arc became a pivotal figure when she led French forces to several victories in 1429 before being captured, tried for heresy, and burned at the stake in 1431. France ultimately won.
questions.memory_hook: Hundred Years' War = England vs France, 116 years. Joan of Arc turned the tide for France at age 17, was burned at 19, and made France win the war. The 'hundred' is approximate.

questions.question: Which country won the first FIFA World Cup in 1930?
questions.explanation: Uruguay hosted and won the first FIFA World Cup in 1930, defeating Argentina 4-2 in the final in Montevideo. Uruguay had also won Olympic gold medals in football in 1924 and 1928, making them the dominant team of the era. Only 13 teams participated in the first tournament — European teams were reluctant to make the long sea voyage to South America.
questions.memory_hook: First World Cup = Uruguay, 1930. Uruguay hosted it and won it — the only time a host nation won the first one. Only 13 teams, Argentina in the final, Uruguay won 4-2.

questions.question: In golf, what term describes completing a hole in one stroke under par?
questions.explanation: A birdie is scoring one stroke under par on a hole. An eagle is two under par, an albatross (or double eagle) is three under par, and a hole-in-one on a par-3 is also an albatross. A bogey is one over par. The term 'birdie' originated in America in 1899 — 'bird' was slang for something excellent, and the term spread through golf.
questions.memory_hook: Birdie = one under par. Eagle = two under. Albatross = three under. Bogey = one over. Golf borrowed bird names for its scoring system — the rarer the shot, the rarer the bird.

questions.question: Which boxer was known as 'The Greatest' and famously said 'Float like a butterfly, sting like a bee'?
questions.explanation: Muhammad Ali (born Cassius Clay) was an Olympic gold medalist (1960) and three-time world heavyweight champion who is widely considered the greatest boxer of all time. He was known for his speed, footwork, and trash-talking poetry. He refused induction into the US military during the Vietnam War, was stripped of his title, and fought his way back to become champion again.
questions.memory_hook: Ali = 'The Greatest.' Float like a butterfly, sting like a bee. Stripped of his title for refusing the Vietnam draft. Won it back twice. The most famous athlete of the 20th century.

questions.question: Which film director is known for Jaws (1975), Raiders of the Lost Ark (1981), and Schindler's List (1993)?
questions.explanation: Steven Spielberg is one of the most commercially successful directors in Hollywood history, having directed blockbusters like Jaws, Close Encounters of the Third Kind, the Indiana Jones series, E.T., Schindler's List, Saving Private Ryan, and many others. He co-founded DreamWorks and has won the Academy Award for Best Director twice (Schindler's List, Saving Private Ryan).
questions.memory_hook: Spielberg = Jaws + Indiana Jones + Schindler's List. He invented the summer blockbuster with Jaws in 1975. He's made more culturally significant films than any other director in history.

questions.question: Adele's album '21' (2011) became one of the best-selling albums in history. Which single from it won Record of the Year at the Grammys?
questions.explanation: 'Rolling in the Deep' won Record of the Year and Song of the Year at the 2012 Grammy Awards. Adele won six Grammy Awards that night — the most wins in a single night by a female artist at the time. '21' sold over 31 million copies worldwide and is one of the best-selling albums of the 21st century.
questions.memory_hook: 'Rolling in the Deep' = Adele's Grammy sweep. Six Grammys in one night. The album '21' was named for her age when she wrote it — a breakup album that the world adopted as its own.

questions.question: Which director created the 'Lord of the Rings' film trilogy (2001-2003)?
questions.explanation: New Zealand director Peter Jackson directed all three Lord of the Rings films, adapted from J.R.R. Tolkien's novels. The trilogy won 17 Academy Awards — The Return of the King won all 11 categories it was nominated in, including Best Picture, matching the record set by Ben-Hur and Titanic. The films were shot simultaneously over 274 days in New Zealand.
questions.memory_hook: Peter Jackson = Lord of the Rings. A New Zealand director shot all three films at once in New Zealand, won 17 Oscars total. Return of the King won 11 out of 11 — a perfect sweep.

questions.question: Which African country contains the most of the Sahara Desert by land area?
questions.explanation: Algeria is the largest country in Africa and contains the largest portion of the Sahara Desert. About 90% of Algeria is covered by the Sahara. Algeria covers 2.38 million square kilometers, making it the largest country in Africa and the 10th largest in the world.
questions.memory_hook: Algeria = most Sahara. 90% of Algeria is desert. It's the biggest country in Africa, mostly because it's mostly desert. The Sahara's heart is in Algeria.

questions.question: Which planet in our solar system is closest to the Sun?
questions.explanation: Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun, orbiting at an average distance of about 58 million km. Despite being closest to the Sun, Mercury is not the hottest planet — that's Venus, which has a thick atmosphere that traps heat. Mercury has almost no atmosphere, so temperature swings wildly: 430°C during the day, -180°C at night.
questions.memory_hook: Mercury = closest to the Sun. Not the hottest — Venus is. No atmosphere means no insulation, so Mercury swings from 430 to -180 degrees. The closest planet has the most extreme temperature swings.
